stem: A 19-year-old client is being monitored for potential compartment syndrome after a crush injury to the right forearm. Based on the documentation, complete the bowtie by selecting the most likely condition, two actions to take, and two parameters to monitor.

physicalExam.general: Anxious, diaphoretic male in obvious pain.
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic, peripheral pulses present but diminished in right arm.
physicalExam.respiratory: Breathing labored, lung sounds clear bilaterally.
physicalExam.neurological: Alert and oriented, severe pain with passive extension of fingers on right hand.
physicalExam.musculoskeletal: Right forearm tense and swollen, skin shiny and pale. Pain out of proportion to exam findings.

clinicalData.radiology: X-Ray Right Forearm: No acute fractures identified. Soft tissue swelling noted in the right forearm compartment. No foreign bodies detected.
nursingOrders: Administer prescribed analgesics and monitor effectiveness.
Elevate the affected extremity at the level of the heart.
Monitor intracompartmental pressures and neurovascular status frequently.
Prepare the client for possible surgical intervention (fasciotomy).

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content: Situation: This is the nurse for the 19-year-old client in room 302 with a right forearm crush injury. At 10:00, his intracompartmental pressure is critical at 38 mmHg, and he reports severe pain with passive finger extension. Background: The client was admitted this morning after a machinery accident. Assessment: Pressures have rapidly increased from 12 mmHg at 08:00. He is tachycardic, hypertensive, and reports 10/10 pain despite IV fentanyl. These findings indicate acute compartment syndrome. Recommendation: I have notified the surgeon. Please prepare for an emergent fasciotomy and maintain NPO status.

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content: General: Anxious, diaphoretic male in obvious pain. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, peripheral pulses present but diminished in right arm. Respiratory: Breathing labored, lung sounds clear bilaterally. Neurological: Alert and oriented, severe pain with passive extension of fingers on right hand. Musculoskeletal: Right forearm tense and swollen, skin shiny and pale. Pain out of proportion to exam findings.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: X-Ray Right Forearm: No acute fractures identified. Soft tissue swelling noted in the right forearm compartment. No foreign bodies detected.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content: Nursing Priorities: 1. Relieve pain and anxiety. 2. Maintain tissue perfusion to the affected extremity. 3. Prevent complications of compartment syndrome. Nursing Interventions: 1. Administer prescribed analgesics and monitor effectiveness. 2. Elevate the affected extremity at the level of the heart. 3. Monitor intracompartmental pressures and neurovascular status frequently. 4. Prepare the client for possible surgical intervention (fasciotomy). 5. Maintain strict NPO status.

actionOptions.text: Prepare the client for emergent fasciotomy
Apply a tight elastic bandage to the arm

hoverRationales.c1: Correct. Increased pressure within a fascial compartment compromises circulation and function. The client's pressure of 38 mmHg, severe pain with passive stretch, and mechanism of injury are classic signs of this surgical emergency.
hoverRationales.c2: Incorrect. This is a potential complication of untreated compartment syndrome, not the primary condition. It involves permanent muscle and nerve damage due to ischemia, which has not yet been established.
hoverRationales.c3: Incorrect. The client's condition is unstable, as evidenced by the rapidly rising compartment pressures and severe, uncontrolled pain. This indicates a pathological process beyond normal inflammation.
hoverRationales.a1: Correct. A fasciotomy surgically opens the fascial compartment to relieve pressure. This is the definitive treatment to restore blood flow and prevent irreversible muscle and nerve damage from ischemia.
hoverRationales.a2: Correct. An emergent fasciotomy is required, necessitating general anesthesia. Maintaining NPO status is a critical safety measure to reduce the risk of pulmonary aspiration of gastric contents during the procedure.
hoverRationales.a3: Incorrect. This action is not indicated and does not address the underlying issue of increased intracompartmental pressure. The client's primary problem is mechanical compression, not a metabolic issue requiring dextrose.
hoverRationales.a4: Incorrect. This action is contraindicated as it would further increase external pressure on the affected compartment, worsening the ischemia and accelerating tissue damage. All constrictive dressings should be removed.
hoverRationales.p1: Correct. Continuously monitoring the pressure provides objective data on the progression of the syndrome and the urgency for intervention. It is the most direct measurement of the underlying pathology.
hoverRationales.p2: Correct. These are key components of a neurovascular assessment. Worsening perfusion, indicated by weak pulses or delayed refill, signals advancing ischemia and the need for immediate intervention.
hoverRationales.p3: Incorrect. Albumin levels are related to oncotic pressure and fluid shifts over time but are not a priority parameter for monitoring an acute, rapidly progressing condition like compartment syndrome.
hoverRationales.p4: Incorrect. While important for monitoring for rhabdomyolysis, a potential complication, the immediate limb-threatening issue is the localized compartment syndrome. Neurovascular status and pressure are the primary focus.

pearls.title: Early Recognition is Key
pearls.description: Compartment syndrome can lead to irreversible muscle and nerve damage if not treated promptly. Pay close attention to the 6 P's: Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, Paralysis, and Pressure.
pearls.strategy: Regularly assess the patient's pain level, neurovascular status, and compartment pressures, especially after trauma or surgery.

pearls.title: Pain Out of Proportion
pearls.description: Severe pain that is not relieved by analgesics and is out of proportion to the injury is a hallmark sign of compartment syndrome.
pearls.strategy: If a patient reports escalating pain despite medication, suspect compartment syndrome and notify the healthcare provider immediately.

traps.title: Misinterpreting Pain
traps.description: Assuming pain is solely due to the initial injury can delay diagnosis and treatment.
traps.strategy: Always consider compartment syndrome in patients at risk, regardless of the initial injury. Investigate disproportionate pain thoroughly.

traps.title: Applying Compression
traps.description: Applying tight bandages or casts can exacerbate compartment syndrome.
traps.strategy: Avoid constrictive dressings in at-risk patients. If a cast is in place, ensure it is not too tight and can be bivalved if necessary.

mnemonics.title: 6 P's of Compartment Syndrome
mnemonics.mnemonic: Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Pressure
mnemonics.description: Remember these key signs and symptoms to quickly assess for compartment syndrome.

mnemonics.title: NPO for Fasciotomy
mnemonics.description: Client needs to be NPO in preparation for possible emergent fasciotomy.
